serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Rattan Pepper Chicken Rice Noodle is approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Rattan Pepper Chicken Rice Noodle (藤椒鸡小锅米线) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010507). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for rattan pepper chicken rice noodle as a composite dish. GI estimated at 61 (medium) based on the GI of rice noodles (fresh/boiled) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (GI range 53–61 for rice noodles). The protein and fat content of the dish (4.96 g and 4.0 g per 100 g respectively) will moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain rice noodles. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 60.48) / 100 ≈ 36.9, rounded to 37.0, based on a 400 g serving providing 60.48 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (182 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving delivers approximately 1296 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — with insufficient potassium (728 mg) and fibre (4 g) to offset the sodium load per DASH criteria; hypertensive individuals should request reduced-broth preparation and limit portion size to 200 g or less.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (estimated 61) rice noodle base with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) providing limited glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 60 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~37, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the low fibre content and medium-GI noodle base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (324 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (4.0 g/100 g) with unknown saturated fat fraction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (1296 mg per 400 g serving) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; saturated fat data are unavailable from the HPB source, so total fat should be treated with caution — individuals with CVD should choose reduced-sodium preparation and keep portions to 200 g or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chili (nightshade) ingredient that cannot be removed; low anti-inflammatory nutrient profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chili peppers are nightshade vegetables whose solanine alkaloids may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; the dish also lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory components (omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre >3 g/100 g), and garlic cannot be removed per the vendor description — RA patients should avoid this dish or request a chili-free, reduced-garlic preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rattan Pepper Chicken Rice Noodle (藤椒鸡小锅米线) is a Chinese-origin noodle soup dish served in Singapore, drawing on Yunnan and Sichuan culinary traditions. It features thin rice vermicelli (米线, mǐ xiàn) simmered in a spiced broth flavoured with rattan pepper (藤椒, téng jiāo) — a green Sichuan-type peppercorn prized for its citrusy, numbing (麻) sensation — alongside sliced or shredded chicken, chili, and garlic. The dish is characterised by a mildly numbing, aromatic heat that is distinct from the intense burn of red chili alone. The broth is typically light but savoury, with soy sauce and sesame oil contributing depth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with 1.56 g sugar and 1.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 324 mg/100 g, making a standard 400 g bowl a high-sodium meal (~1296 mg). The glycaemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the rice noodle base. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension due to high sodium and low potassium/fibre offset, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the medium-GI noodle base and low fibre, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to sodium load. The dish contains garlic that cannot be removed and chili (nightshade), making it unsuitable for RA-sensitive individuals without modification. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010507) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the HPB canonical database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010507) as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this dish entry. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. The dish description notes garlic cannot be removed — this is preserved as an allergen/intolerance flag. 5. Input field name 'Rattan Papper' is a known spelling variant (typo for 'Pepper') preserved in aliases for retrieval coverage. 6. Halal status assessed as likely true given chicken and plant-based ingredients, but vendor confirmation is recommended.
